text: Robotic eight millimeter trocars are placed in a straight line from the left upper quadrant to the right lower quadrant, with an additional trocar positioned in the left lower quadrant.
The surgeon begins dissection by exploring the ileocolic pedicle, followed by opening the ileocolic window to access the underlying structures.
The surgeon isolates the ileocolic vessels from the superior mesenteric artery and vein, ligates them with Hem-o-lok clips, and divides them.

The surgeon performs mesenteric dissection using a medial to lateral approach, carefully navigating between the embryological planes anterior to the adenoma. The right colic artery is then dissected, ligated, and divided.
The surgeon identifies the right branch of the middle colic artery, ligates it, and divides it using surgical instruments.

The surgeon takes down the lateral attachments of the transverse colon. After completing the mesenteric dissection, the transverse colon is prepared and transected using a robotic stapler with a single fire.
The surgeon prepares the terminal ileum for transection and performs the transection using a robotic stapler, completing the separation of the ileum.

The surgeon places a stay suture to prepare for the side-to-side anastomosis. Small incisions are made in the transverse colon and terminal ileum for the insertion of the robotic linear stapler. An intracorporeal side-to-side ileocolic anastomosis is created, and the opening in the anastomosis is closed using a 3-0 Vicryl suture.

The surgeon places the specimen into an endobag and extracts it through a five-centimeter suprapubic incision. The intact specimen is then displayed, and the distance between the tumor and the high vascular tie is measured.
